posts.title: How I developed my very own Portfolio Website using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript
posts.author: Syed Ishtiyaq Ahmed
posts.summary: Have you ever felt overwhelmed by the extensive use of frameworks and advanced technologies to build a simple portfolio website? Same here, which is why I chose to keep things simple by developing it using only _HTML_, _CSS_, and _JavaScript_.

posts.title: Rethinking Focus Apps: An Awareness-Based Approach Instead of Blocking
posts.author: Syed Ishtiyaq Ahmed
posts.summary: Most focus tools rely on blocking distractions, but this approach often fails to address the root cause. This article explores an awareness-based system that helps users recognize attention shifts and make intentional decisions instead of enforcing restrictions.
posts.content:
## Introduction

Many focus tools aim to block specific websites or applications. While this method can be effective in some cases, it tends to view distraction as an element that needs to be eliminated completely.

However, sometimes distraction may not arise due to the possibility of accessing certain websites or applications. Distraction can be a shift in attention that happens during work. If the user does not notice this transition, then it becomes easy for them to engage in activities that were not initially intended.

This article discusses a new way of addressing this problem. Rather than preventing distractions, it proposes that these distractions become apparent. In essence, the proposed system introduces a brief pause during which users can observe the transition of attention and decide whether to return to their task or continue.

To test this theory, I created a desktop application titled Intent.

---

## Problem with Focus Tools

The blocking strategy is applied by many tools which prohibit access to some websites and applications. Even though it allows avoiding possible distractors, it does not resolve the source of distractions.

Users tend to adjust themselves to this strategy. They can choose another application, postpone their task, or even turn off the program if necessary. In such situations, the tool acts as a temporary solution rather than a permanent one.

There is another drawback associated with a static list of prohibited websites. The definition of a distraction can vary from one task to another.

An application might be helpful for one job but not for others. This means that static rules cannot meet the needs of each situation.

Finally, there is a tendency toward shifting control from users to tools. The tool limits freedom of choice, which is essential for understanding how people use their time.

Taking all these drawbacks into account, blocking alone is not enough to solve the problem. Another approach is to focus on the moment when attention changes and help users identify that point.

---

## Awareness-Based Approach

An alternative approach focuses on awareness at the moment distraction occurs. Unlike blocking, awareness does not restrict access but instead detects contextual changes and initiates a brief reminder.

In other words, this strategy supports decision-making rather than enforcing rules. The responsibility remains with the user, who has chosen to switch attention.

The key idea is to remind the user in a non-invasive way that they might be getting distracted while working.

When a user switches to an application outside the defined scope:

- The system does not react immediately
- A short delay ensures the action was intentional
- If the user remains in that application, a reminder appears

---

## How It Works

The process is simple and combines contextual detection with minimal interruption:

### Define Scope

The user selects apps relevant to their current task. This becomes the "in-scope" set.

### Start Session

The application runs in the background and tracks the active app.

### Detect Context Switch

When a new app becomes active, the system checks if it is within the selected scope.

### Evaluate Distraction

If the app is outside the scope:

- The system waits briefly
- This avoids interrupting quick or intentional switches

### Trigger Reminder

If the user stays outside the scope, a notification appears asking if the switch was intentional.

---

## Open Source

The project is open-source, and contributions are welcome in several areas:

- Improving monitoring accuracy
- Enhancing the user interface
- Refining workflows

Feedback is also encouraged, especially regarding usability and system behavior in real-world scenarios.

The codebase is designed with:

- Readability
- Extensibility
- Modular structure (core logic, UI, utilities)

The goal is to keep the system simple while allowing gradual improvements.

---

## Links

### Website

https://sdishtiyaqahmed.github.io/intent-focus-web/

### Source Code

https://github.com/SdIshtiyaqAhmed/intent-focus

posts.title: Sustainable Cooling: Telangana Cool Roof Policy 2023-2028
posts.author: Syed Ishtiyaq Ahmed
posts.summary: With rising temperatures and increasing energy demand, Telangana’s Cool Roof Policy (2023–2028) promotes heat-reflective materials as a simple, cost-effective solution to reduce indoor heat and environmental impact.
posts.content:
## Introduction

Global temperatures are rising and are above normal, especially in India. There have been summers with high temperatures recorded in most places in recent times. To counter the growing temperatures, people are depending on electronic cooling devices such as coolers and air conditioners.

Due to this, the electricity demand in summer is skyrocketing, and consumers are paying large amounts in electricity bills. Additionally, to meet this increased demand, more resources such as coal and fossil fuels are extracted to generate electricity, which negatively impacts the environment.

This situation resembles the concept of the "Tragedy of the Commons", where the common resource is the atmosphere, and the tragedy is global warming.

---

## Passive Cooling as a Solution

There are countermeasures to cool down the inside of a building without using electricity or harming the environment. One such measure is the use of heat-reflective materials on building surfaces.

These materials can reflect up to 80% of sunlight, helping to reduce indoor temperatures by approximately 2 to 5 degrees Celsius. It is a simple, cost-effective, easy-to-use, and non-technical solution that improves comfort while being environmentally friendly.

---

## Telangana Cool Roof Policy 2023-2028

The Government of Telangana has introduced a cool roof policy to promote and support the use of heat-reflective materials on rooftops to reduce indoor temperatures.

The policy aims to cover 300 square kilometres of roof area across the state by 2028, as outlined by the Municipal Administration and Urban Development Department.

This initiative also focuses on raising awareness about the benefits of heat-reflective materials. Many homeowners and builders are either unaware of this solution or believe that it is expensive and ineffective.

---

## Positive Externality

This initiative is a strong example of a positive externality. If individuals adopt heat-reflective materials on their rooftops, the overall use of electronic cooling devices such as air conditioners and coolers can decrease.

This reduction lowers electricity demand and decreases reliance on fossil fuels, ultimately benefiting the environment and society as a whole.

In this way, individual actions contribute to a collective good, making sustainable cooling an effective and scalable solution.

posts.title: Tragedy of the Commons: Streets and Parking
posts.author: Syed Ishtiyaq Ahmed
posts.summary: Improper roadside parking in urban areas reflects the tragedy of the commons, where shared spaces are overused. Structured planning and valuing parking spaces can help reduce congestion and improve urban life.
posts.content:
## Introduction

In urban areas, many places lack proper parking facilities, and people often park their vehicles on the roadside. This can lead to traffic congestion and an increase in road rage incidents.

In many cases, despite the existence of fines (challans) and regulations, the problem persists due to gaps in enforcement, infrastructure limitations, and poor civic sense among the public.

A structured approach to street usage—dividing space into footpaths, parking zones, and commercial areas—can significantly improve the quality of urban life.

---

## Street as a "Common" Resource

Streets are maintained by the government or local authorities and are used collectively by the public. Ideally, people use these spaces based on a shared understanding of order and fairness.

However, individual behavior does not always align with collective goals, leading to inefficient use of shared resources.

---

## The Unassigned Parking "Tragedy"

An individual may park their vehicle on the roadside assuming that a single car will not significantly impact traffic flow.

But when many individuals act on the same assumption, the result is overcrowding of streets. This reduces available road space, narrows traffic movement, and leads to severe congestion.

This is where the parking-related tragedy unfolds.

---

## Tragedy of the Commons

When parking is treated as a free and common resource, it becomes prone to overuse and misuse.

To address this, parking spaces must be recognized as valuable resources. Assigning economic value through paid parking systems—already implemented in many countries—can discourage improper use.

By treating streets as regulated and valuable assets rather than free-for-all spaces, cities can reduce congestion, improve mobility, and create a more organized urban environment.

posts.title: The Rise of Artificial Intelligence and Education
posts.author: Syed Ishtiyaq Ahmed
posts.summary: Artificial Intelligence is transforming education by enabling personalised learning, automation, and accessibility. This article explores the history of education, evolution of AI, its role in learning, and the challenges that come with it.
posts.content:
## Introduction

Imagine a society where every student has their own dedicated teacher. Sounds unlikely, right? With advancements in Artificial Intelligence, this idea is no longer just a dream—it is becoming increasingly possible.

This article explores the history of education, the evolution of Artificial Intelligence, its role in modern learning, and the challenges associated with it.

---

## History of Education

### Early Beginnings

In the earliest stages of human society, there was no formal education system—no books, classrooms, or structured learning. Knowledge was passed down through practical experience and teachings from elders.

### Ancient Civilisations

Around 3000 BCE, formal education systems began to emerge. Students learned reading, writing, mathematics, arts, science, and philosophy.

### Medieval Period

Between the 5th and 15th centuries, the first universities were established, marking the beginning of structured higher education and research.

### The Printing Revolution

In the 15th century, the invention of the printing press made knowledge more accessible and affordable, enabling mass distribution of books.

### Age of Public Education

Between the 17th and 20th centuries, governments began implementing public education systems, making learning more accessible to larger populations.

### Introduction of Technology

In the 20th century, technologies such as radio, television, and computers were introduced as educational tools.

### Digital Learning

In the 21st century, online learning emerged, enabling global connectivity between teachers and students.

---

## Evolution of Artificial Intelligence

### Early Ideas

In the 1950s, researchers envisioned machines capable of thinking and learning. Early AI systems could solve simple problems and play basic games.

### Growing Capabilities

Over time, computers improved in pattern recognition, language processing, and instruction-following, although still limited by hardware constraints.

### The Rise of AI

In the 21st century, advancements in computing power and data availability enabled AI to perform complex tasks and become useful across various sectors, including education.

---

## Role of AI in Education

### Personalised Learning

AI enables tailored learning experiences based on individual student needs, effectively simulating a one-to-one teacher-student interaction.

### Automation of Administrative Tasks

Tasks such as attendance tracking, grading, lesson planning, and exam proctoring can be automated using AI.

### Accessible Education

AI-powered platforms make education more affordable and accessible to students worldwide.

### Real-Time Feedback

Students can receive instant feedback and recommendations, improving learning efficiency.

### Interactive Content

AI can generate engaging and interactive educational materials that enhance understanding and retention.

---

## Challenges of AI in Education

### Data Privacy and Security

The collection and storage of student data raise concerns about privacy and potential data breaches.

### Bias and Fairness

AI systems may produce biased outcomes depending on the data they are trained on, which can affect fairness in education.

### Technical and Ethical Concerns

Technical failures may disrupt learning, and ethical issues may arise in human-AI interactions.

---

## Conclusion

With the rise of the internet, education has become more accessible and affordable worldwide. AI has the potential to further enhance the quality of education by offering personalised and efficient learning experiences.

Although challenges exist, they can be mitigated through awareness, strong policies, and transparent implementation. When integrated thoughtfully, AI can become a powerful component of the future education system.
